response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "I've been a carpenter for over fifty years, and I believe that everyone deserves to be judged on their own abilities. Stereotype threat seems like one of those modern terms they throw around, but from my perspective, it's just common sense really.\n\nWhen people hear the word 'stereotype', I think about all the prejudice we're trying to overcome in society. In STEM fields, there might be expectations or biases that certain groups can't succeed because of their background. For example, if someone is told that women aren't as good at math as men are, and then they face a challenging problem in front of others who have those stereotypes, I imagine the pressure must hold them back.\n\nAs an older woman, I've seen my own share of unfairness throughout life without even thinking about STEM specifically. In my trade, there was always this idea that carpenters were mostly men and not many women wanted to be or could break into it because people thought it wasn't for us. But when I started working with my hands at a young age, learning blueprints and measurements through trial and error while helping out in the shop every weekend growing up—my dad didn't have formal education but he was practical—and proving myself time after time, stereotypes fell apart.\n\nI think we definitely should address stereotype threat because letting harmful assumptions go unchallenged hurts real people. It keeps talented minds from exploring their potential regardless of gender or color. Maybe through mentorship programs where experienced workers like me guide newcomers without making them feel like they're exceptions?",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on my life experiences as a carpenter and woman, I know firsthand how stereotypes can affect confidence and performance in technical fields. Growing up with limited parental education but excelling anyway shows that capability isn't tied to background.",
  "tone": "Pragmatic yet empathetic based on personal experience",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields":["age", "gender", "occupation", "city_of_living", "education_level", "parents_education.parent_1", "parents_education.parent_2"]
}

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been a carpenter for over fifty years, and I believe that everyone deserves to be judged on their own abilities. Stereotype threat seems like one of those modern terms they throw around, but from my perspective, it's just common sense really.

When people hear the word 'stereotype', I think about all the prejudice we're trying to overcome in society. In STEM fields, there might be expectations or biases that certain groups can't succeed because of their background. For example, if someone is told that women aren't as good at math as men are, and then they face a challenging problem in front of others who have those stereotypes, I imagine the pressure must hold them back.

As an older woman, I've seen my own share of unfairness throughout life without even thinking about STEM specifically. In my trade, there was always this idea that carpenters were mostly men and not many women wanted to be or could break into it because people thought it wasn't for us. But when I started working with my hands at a young age, learning blueprints and measurements through trial and error while helping out in the shop every weekend growing up—my dad didn't have formal education but he was practical—and proving myself time after time, stereotypes fell apart.

I think we definitely should address stereotype threat because letting harmful assumptions go unchallenged hurts real people. It keeps talented minds from exploring their potential regardless of gender or color. Maybe through mentorship programs where experienced workers like me guide newcomers without making them feel like they're exceptions?
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my life experiences as a carpenter and woman, I know firsthand how stereotypes can affect confidence and performance in technical fields. Growing up with limited parental education but excelling anyway shows that capability isn't tied to background.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic yet empathetic based on personal experience
